- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- artifact-design: Design guidance and fundamentals for Artifacts.
- artifact-capabilities: Runtime capabilities a published Artifact can declare — calling the user's claude.ai connectors (MCP) from the page, and future abilities. Load this BEFORE passing `capabilities` to the Artifact tool or writing any `window.claude.mcp` code.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

1	# Clean-room reimplementation protocol — tamarin-rs relicensing
2	
3	Purpose: independently recreate three modules of tamarin-rs (wellformedness
4	checker, graph/dot pipeline, web layer) without access to GPL-covered
5	expression, so the results are original works relicensable at will.
6	
7	## Rooms
8	- DIRTY ROOM (Kevin Milner + main-session assistant): has read the GPL Haskell
9	  source and the ported Rust modules. Produces only: interoperability headers
10	  (type surfaces, entry-point signatures, comments stripped), oracle wrappers,
11	  and behavioral bug reports. Never contributes implementation text.
12	- CLEAN ROOM (isolated agents, one per cluster): receive ONLY the materials
13	  listed per cluster below. Their complete tool-call transcripts are retained
14	  as evidence of non-access.
15	
16	## Clean-room materials (exhaustive)
17	1. This protocol, the cluster SPEC.md, and interface/ headers (type surfaces +
18	   boundary signatures only).
19	2. Black-box oracles: `wf_oracle.sh` (runs the compiled Haskell binary on an
20	   input file), `hs_server.sh` (serves a theory in the Haskell web UI for HTTP
21	   probing), pre-captured oracle outputs (oracle/captures/,
22	   oracle/captured_responses/), and GPL example .spthy files used solely as
23	   observation INPUTS.
24	3. Public Rust toolchain and general documentation.
25	
26	## Forbidden to the clean room
27	- Reading ANY file under /home/kamilner/tamarin-rs/ — source, comments, git
28	  history — with the sole exceptions of executing the oracle scripts and
29	  reading oracle/captured_responses/ (captured program OUTPUT, not source).
30	- Fetching tamarin-prover source, docs describing its internals, or its GitHub.
31	- Reproducing any tamarin-prover implementation detail from model memory:
32	  every behavior must be derived from the oracle, the captures, or the SPEC.
33	  Exact output strings (warning texts, DOT syntax, HTML) MUST be taken from
34	  observed oracle output — that is compatibility content, not expression.
35	
36	## Audit & integration (after implementation)
37	1. Transcript audit: verify from the retained agent transcripts that no
38	   forbidden path was accessed.
39	2. Similarity audit: a reviewer (may see both sides) compares the clean code
40	   against the Haskell originals; flagged similarities are sent back as
41	   redo-instructions, never patched directly.
42	3. Behavioral acceptance: byte-parity corpus must pass.
43	4. Integration: dirty room may write thin adapters and mechanical renames to
44	   fit the workspace API, but must not transplant logic from the replaced
45	   modules. The replaced modules are deleted; their GPL-pending headers go
46	   with them (history retains them under GPL).
47

1	# Round 3 (Unit A): handler semantics (the UI state machine)
2	
3	Extend workspace/web-clean from templates to BEHAVIOR: the handler logic that
4	decides WHAT each route returns. Sources: the 81 manifests (your route census
5	covers 48k routes) + live probing (hs_server.sh is buggy; use your own
6	--port=<n> wrapper as before; ports 3100-3199 are yours).
7	Model, byte-exact where captures show determinism:
8	1. Proof-tree traversal semantics: next/prev ordering over proof states,
9	   path encoding, version increments across edits/autoprove.
10	2. autoprove variants (the a/b/all/characterization route forms in the
11	   census): which proof mutation each performs and the response envelope.
12	3. proof-step application responses, del/path semantics, add-lemma flow.
13	4. The two integration blockers: (a) the NON-LOCAL-origin page shell (the
14	   served shell differs when the client is not 127.0.0.1 — probe by
15	   connecting to the server via the machine's non-loopback interface IP);
16	   (b) dynamic edit-form textarea rows= (probe lemmas of varying line counts
17	   to find the rows formula).
18	ARCHITECTURE: parameterize over a ProverOps callback trait (parse theory,
19	apply proof method at path, autoprove, pretty-print fragments) — the ported
20	prover supplies these at integration; you implement the state machine, route
21	dispatch decisions, and response assembly through your existing templates.
22	Update BEHAVIOR.md/QUERIES.log; tests with capture fixtures; REPORT3.md +
23	10-line summary.

No prover source was read. 6 7 Terms: **web layer** = the code that dispatches routes, escapes, and wraps 8 strings into envelopes/pages. **prover fragment** = a string the prover produced 9 (pretty-printed terms, constraint systems, method names, DOT bodies) that the 10 web layer embeds opaquely. This spec pins down the web layer; prover fragments 11 are treated as inputs. 12 13 --- 14 15 ## 1. Route grammar 16 17 Every dynamic route has the shape: 18 19 ``` 20 /thy//// 21 ``` 22 23 * `` — analysis kind. Only `trace` observed (a `diff` kind is 24 plausible but never appears in the corpus). 25 * `` — theory **version** selector. In real requests it is a decimal 26 number. The manifests store it as the literal token `#`; live probing shows 27 `%23` returns **404**, so `#` is the crawl tool's placeholder meaning "the 28 server's current version", and every URL the server *emits* uses the resolved 29 numeric index. Applying a proof method creates a new version, so the index 30 seen in a response's links is generally larger than the one requested. 31 * `` — selects the response family: 32 33 | handler | body kind | notes | 34 |-----------------------------|-----------|-------| 35 | `main/…` | JSON | AJAX content pane; envelope `{html,title}` or `{redirect}` | 36 | `overview/…` | HTML | full theory-view page (see §5) | 37 | `intdot/…` | HTML | graph mini-page embedding `` (§8) | 38 | `interactive-graph-def/…` | DOT | Graphviz source for a proof node (§8) | 39 | `next/…`, `prev/…` | text | a single navigation URL (§9) | 40 | `autoprove/…` | JSON | `{redirect}`; **text** with HTTP status 0 on timeout | 41 | `source`, `message` | text | the theory source, verbatim (§9) | 42 43 `main/*` sub-handlers (observed, exhaustive over the corpus): 44 45 | sub-route | meaning | 46 |-----------|---------| 47 | `main/help` | theory header + wellformedness report + keyboard help | 48 | `main/message` | signature + construction/deconstruction rules | 49 | `main/rules` | multiset rewriting rules (+ restrictions) | 50 | `main/tactic` | tactic(s) listing | 51 | `main/cases/{raw\|refined}/{level}/{n}` | source cases | 52 | `main/lemma/{name}` | lemma summary | 53 | `main/proof/{lemma}/{path…}` | constraint system + applicable methods at a proof node; `path` is a `/`-joined sequence of case-name segments, root `_` | 54 | `main/method/{lemma}/{n}` | apply method `n` → `{redirect}` | 55 | `main/add/{pos}` | add-lemma form (`pos` = a lemma name or `` ⇒ `%3Cfirst%3E`) | 56 | `main/edit/{name}` | edit-lemma form | 57 | `main/delete/{name}` | delete-lemma confirmation | 58 59 `overview/*` mirrors `main/*` for the pages that render a full shell: 60 `overview/help`, `overview/proof/{lemma}/{path…}`. `intdot/*` and 61 `interactive-graph-def/*` take the same `proof/{lemma}/{path…}` (and 62 `cases/…`) tails. 63 64 ### Authoritative handler/kind census (all 81 manifests, 48824 dynamic routes) 65 66 Enumerated with a template pass ([Q020]–[Q021]); the response `kind` is fixed 67 per handler: 68 69 | handler | kind | count | argument shape | 70 |--------------------------|-------------|-------|----------------| 71 | `main` | json | 17609 | see `main/*` table above | 72 | `interactive-graph-def` | dot | 14705 | `proof/{lemma}[/{path…}]` | 73 | `intdot` | html | 14705 | `proof/{lemma}[/{path…}]` | 74 | `overview` | html | 473 | `help` \| `proof/{lemma}[/{path…}]` | 75 | `next` | text | 392 | `normal/proof/{lemma}` | 76 | `prev` | text | 392 | `normal/proof/{lemma}` | 77 | `autoprove` | json (text on timeout) | 392 | `idfs/{bound}/False/proof/{lemma}` | 78 | `source` | text | 81 | (none) | 79 | `message` | text | 81 | (none) | 80 81 `autoprove/idfs/{bound}/False/proof/{lemma}` is the observed autoprove request 82 shape (`idfs` = the search strategy, `{bound}` a numeric depth bound, `False` a 83 flag). It answers with a `{redirect}` to the resolved `overview/proof/{lemma}` 84 whose index is **bumped** by the applied proof — live probe [L6] shows 85 `autoprove/idfs/0/False/proof/types` on a version-1 theory returning 86 `{"redirect":"/thy/trace/2/overview/proof/nonce_secrecy"}`. On the 6 corpus 87 timeouts the same route instead returns HTTP status 0 with a text body. 88 89 `next`/`prev` are pure text: a single navigation URL. The target need not be a 90 proof node — live probe shows `prev/normal/proof/types` (the first lemma) 91 returning `/thy/trace/1/main/cases/refined/0/0`. 92 93 Content-types (live probe [L6]): json = `application/json; charset=utf-8`, 94 text = `text/plain; charset=utf-8`, html = `text/html; charset=utf-8`. 95 96 Static/other routes seen in emitted links (not in the crawl of dynamic content): 97 `GET /` (index), `POST /` (upload), `POST /thy/…/reload`, 98 `GET /thy/…/download/{file}`, `POST /thy/…/get_and_append/{file}`, 99 `GET /thy/…/edit/{edit|add|delete}/{name}` (form POST targets), 100 `/static/**`, `/static/LICENSE`, `/static/img/tamarin-logo-3-0-0.png`. 101 102 Unmatched paths → **404** full HTML page (§7). 103 104 --- 105 106 ## 2. Response envelopes 107 108 ### JSON (`main/*`, `method/*`, `autoprove/*`) 109 Exactly two compact shapes, keys in this order, **no** insignificant whitespace, 110 **no** trailing newline: 111 112 ``` 113 {"html":,"title":} — content pane 114 {"redirect":} — client should navigate to the URL 115 ``` 116 117 JSON string escaping is standard: only `"` `\` and control chars are escaped; 118 `/ < > &` are **not** escaped; non-ASCII is emitted as **literal UTF-8** (no 119 `\uXXXX`). `serde_json`'s default output reproduces this byte-for-byte 120 (verified on 2450 distinct bodies). 121 122 ### HTML (`overview/*`, `intdot/*`, `/`, 404) 123 `text/html; charset=utf-8`. Full documents beginning `\n…` 124 and ending `` with **no** trailing newline. 125 126 ### text (`source`, `message`, `next`, `prev`) 127 `text/plain`. Returned verbatim; `source`/`message` end at the theory's final 128 `end` with no trailing newline; `next`/`prev` are a bare URL path. 129 130 ### DOT (`interactive-graph-def/*`) 131 Graphviz source; the empty-graph skeleton ends with `}\n`. 132 133 --- 134 135 ## 3. Escaping rules 136 137 The web layer's HTML text escape maps five characters and passes everything else 138 (including all prover-emitted unicode operators) through unchanged: 139 140 ``` 141 & -> & 142 < -> < 143 > -> > 144 " -> " 145 ' -> ' 146 ``` 147 148 Applied to: the "loaded from" path, lemma names/text in the edit form, the add 149 form's position marker (`` → `<first>` in the form action), the 150 echoed path in the 404 page. #!/usr/bin/env bash
# Black-box oracle: serve a .spthy in the HS interactive web UI.
# Usage: hs_server.sh start <file.spthy> <port>   (then curl http://127.0.0.1:<port>/...)
#        hs_server.sh stop <port>
export PATH="/home/linuxbrew/.linuxbrew/bin:$PATH"
case "$1" in
  start) d=$(mktemp -d); cp "$2" "$d/"; nohup "/home/kamilner/tamarin-rs/tamarin-prover-testing/.stack-work/install/x86_64-linux-tinfo6/ec0cb11b1bfcf8776d45e0357bbc6d6ff2077f9222735af22115429c8cdfcef1/9.6.7/bin/tamarin-prover" interactive "$d" --port "$3" >/tmp/hs_server_$3.log 2>&1 & echo $! > /tmp/hs_server_$3.pid; sleep 4; echo "up on $3 (pid $(cat /tmp/hs_server_$3.pid))";;
  stop) kill $(cat /tmp/hs_server_$2.pid) 2>/dev/null; rm -f /tmp/hs_server_$2.pid; echo stopped;;
esac

It reads an NDJSON extract of every `kind == "html"` body — one record 39 `{mf, name, ver, file, u, b}` per line — renders each body from the crate's own 40 templates, and tallies byte-exact reproduction per family. 41 42 Reproduce the extract (fast; jq streams each manifest, ~33 s total over 1.8 GB): 43 44 ``` 45 cd oracle/captured_responses 46 for F in *.hs.json; do mf="${F%%.*}"; jq -c --arg mf "${mf:0:12}" ' 47 (.manifest["/thy/trace/#/overview/help"].body) as $ov 48 | ($ov|capture("Theory: (?<n>[^<]*)")|.n) as $name 49 | ($ov|capture("Tamarin (?[^<]*)")|.v) as $ver 50 | ($ov|capture("/download/(?[^>]*)>Download")|.f) as $file 51 | .manifest|to_entries[]|select(.value.kind=="html") 52 | {mf:$mf,name:$name,ver:$ver,file:$file,u:.key,b:.value.body}' "$F"; done \ 53 > html_corpus.ndjson # 15178 lines 54 cargo run --release --example corpus_html -- html_corpus.ndjson 55 ``` 56 57 The per-manifest `name`/`ver`/`file` are taken **once** from that manifest's 58 `overview/help` page (a *sibling* artifact), never from the body under test. 59 Version is `1.13.0` in all 81 manifests. 60 61 A committed 20-record subset (`tests/fixtures/html_sample.ndjson`, 6 theories, 62 indices 1/3/8/16, both families) is checked permanently by 63 `html_page_generality_sample_byte_identical` in `cargo test`, so generality is 64 CI-enforced without the 620 MB corpus extract. 65 66 ## Family 1 — `intdot/*` (14705, fully model-driven) 67 68 Each body is the fixed mini-page template with exactly two variable slots: the 69 theory name (in `Theory: NAME`) and the full DOT source path 70 `dotsrc="/thy/trace/{IDX}/interactive-graph-def/{TAIL}"`. The render is 71 `render_intdot(NAME, dotsrc_path(IDX, TAIL))`. 72 73 Provenance of the three inputs, and why the byte match is **not** circular: 74 * `NAME` — from the manifest's `overview/help` title (sibling page). Because the 75 render uses the sibling name and still matches, the name placement is proven. 76 An independent guard also asserts the body's own title equals the sibling name 77 (**0 failures** over 14705). 78 * `TAIL` — taken from the **request URL key** (everything after `/intdot/`). The 79 server's transform is "swap handler `intdot`→`interactive-graph-def`, keep the 80 tail". An independent guard asserts the body's emitted dotsrc tail equals the 81 URL-key tail (**0 failures** over 14705) — so the tail is a genuine passthrough, 82 not something read back from the target. 83 * `IDX` — the theory-version index. The capture tool normalised every URL key's 84 index to the literal `#`, so the request index is the **one** value the keys do 85 not preserve; it is recovered from the emitted dotsrc. Indices genuinely vary 86 per node (e.g. manifest `d381…` emits both `1` and `16`, because the crawler 87 captured some proof nodes before and some after autoprove advanced the version) 88 — this is a request/theory-state parameter, not a web-layer choice. Of the 83, the diffs are exactly (a) the version 131 integer inside emitted links and (b) proof-state-dependent constraint-system 132 content — both prover fragments — with no template byte ever differing. So the 133 web-layer rule "center = corresponding `main/*` html + one trailing space" holds 134 wherever the two captures share a version; the shortfall is a property of the 135 crawl, not of the renderer. Independently, the harness confirms the center pane 136 ends in `" "` for **473/473** overview pages. 137 138 ### The WEST pane (the honest residual) 139 140 `WEST_INNER` (the proof-script listing: theory header, item links, every lemma 141 with its `by sorry` / proof-tree) is web-layer-generated by `proofscript.rs` and 142 is identical across all overview pages of a given theory *version*. It is the one 143 pane not reconstructed from an independent artifact in this round: generating it 144 for an arbitrary theory needs the theory's full item/lemma/proof-tree model, 145 which exists (byte-reproduced) only for the Chaum theory so far (the committed 146 `proof_script_solved_tree_byte_identical` test drives 40 proof lines with 147 branching cases/next/qed and by-steps). For the other 70 theories the west pane 148 is treated here as an opaque per-(theory,version) fragment. Closing it fully 149 means parsing each theory's proof model — the natural next round. 150 151 ## Tests 152 153 `cargo test` → **38 passing** (19 unit + 19 integration/parity), `cargo clippy 154 --tests --examples` clean. 1	//! The full-page theory-view HTML shell returned by the `overview/*` routes.
2	//!
3	//! Observed structure (constant across theories after substituting the four
4	//! parameters below): a fixed `<head>` of stylesheet/script links, a north
5	//! header bar, then four layout panes — west "Proof scripts", east "Debug
6	//! information" (always empty), and center "Visualization display". The west
7	//! pane embeds the proof-script markup (see [`crate::proofscript`]); the center
8	//! pane embeds the currently-selected main content HTML (the same HTML the
9	//! corresponding `main/*` route returns in its JSON `html` field).
10	//!
11	//! Scaffolding constants in `shell_template` are byte-exact copies of oracle
12	//! output with four `§`-delimited slots: NAME, IDX, VERSION, FILENAME. The
13	//! body has no trailing newline (ends `</html>`). All internal links use the
14	//! resolved numeric theory index, never `#`.
15	
16	use crate::escape::html_escape;
17	use crate::shell_template::{PAGE_MID, PAGE_PREFIX, PAGE_TAIL};
18	
19	/// Parameters that vary between rendered pages.
20	pub struct PageParams<'a> {
21	    /// Theory name, shown in `<title>Theory: NAME</title>`.
22	    pub theory_name: &'a str,
23	    /// Resolved numeric theory index used in every internal URL.
24	    pub index: u64,
25	    /// Tamarin version string shown in the header (e.g. `"1.13.0"`).
26	    pub version: &'a str,
27	    /// Source filename used in the download / append links (e.g. `"foo.spthy"`).
28	    pub filename: &'a str,
29	}
30	
31	/// Render the full theory-view page from the shell parameters plus the already
32	/// rendered west (proof-script) and center (main content) pane inner HTML.
33	pub fn render_page(p: &PageParams, west_inner: &str, center_inner: &str) -> String {
34	    let idx = p.index.to_string();
35	    let prefix = PAGE_PREFIX
36	        .replace("§NAME§", &html_escape(p.theory_name))
37	        .replace("§IDX§", &idx)
38	        .replace("§VERSION§", p.version)
39	        .replace("§FILENAME§", p.filename);
40	    let mut out = String::with_capacity(
41	        prefix.len() + west_inner.len() + PAGE_MID.len() + center_inner.len() + PAGE_TAIL.len(),
42	    );
43	    out.push_str(&prefix);
44	    out.push_str(west_inner);
45	    out.push_str(PAGE_MID);
46	    out.push_str(center_inner);
47	    out.push_str(PAGE_TAIL);
48	    out
49	}

===wl_server.sh===
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# Clean-room live-probe wrapper (black-box execution of the sanctioned oracle
# binary only). Works around the hs_server.sh --port bug by using --port=<n>.
export PATH="/home/linuxbrew/.linuxbrew/bin:$PATH"
BIN="/home/kamilner/tamarin-rs/tamarin-prover-testing/.stack-work/install/x86_64-linux-tinfo6/ec0cb11b1bfcf8776d45e0357bbc6d6ff2077f9222735af22115429c8cdfcef1/9.6.7/bin/tamarin-prover"
case "$1" in
  start)
    d=$(mktemp -d); cp "$2" "$d/"
    nohup "$BIN" interactive "$d" --port="$3" >/tmp/wl_server_$3.log 2>&1 &
    echo $! > /tmp/wl_server_$3.pid
    sleep 5
    echo "up on $3 (pid $(cat /tmp/wl_server_$3.pid))"
    ;;
  stop)
    kill "$(cat /tmp/wl_server_$2.pid)" 2>/dev/null
    rm -f /tmp/wl_server_$2.pid
    echo stopped
    ;;
esac

1	//! The `intdot/*` mini-page and the `interactive-graph-def/*` DOT skeleton.
2	//!
3	//! `intdot/<path>` returns a tiny standalone HTML page whose only content is a
4	//! `<dot-graph-viz>` custom element pointing at the matching
5	//! `interactive-graph-def/<path>` DOT source (same trailing path, resolved
6	//! numeric index). No trailing newline (ends `</html>`).
7	//!
8	//! `interactive-graph-def/<path>` returns Graphviz DOT. For a proof node with
9	//! no constructed graph (e.g. a freshly opened lemma) the body is the fixed
10	//! empty-graph skeleton below, which DOES end in a newline. Non-empty graphs
11	//! are prover-produced and out of scope for this template.
12	
13	const INTDOT_TEMPLATE: &str = r#"<!DOCTYPE html>
14	<html><head><meta charset="UTF-8" /><meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1.0" /><title>Theory: §NAME§</title><style> body,html{width: 100%; height: 100%; overflow: hidden; margin: 0; padding: 0; }</style><link rel="stylesheet" href="/static/css/intdot-style.css"><script type="module" src="/static/js/intdot-graph.es.js"></script></script></head><body><dot-graph-viz dotsrc="§DOTSRC§"></dot-graph-viz>
15	</body></html>"#;
16	
17	/// The empty-graph DOT skeleton returned for a proof node with no graph.
18	pub const EMPTY_GRAPH_DOT: &str = "digraph \"G\" {\nnodesep=\"0.3\";\nranksep=\"0.3\";\nnode[fontsize=\"8\",fontname=\"Helvetica\",width=\"0.3\",height=\"0.2\"];\nedge[fontsize=\"8\",fontname=\"Helvetica\"];\n\n}\n";
19	
20	/// Render the `intdot` mini HTML page.
21	///
22	/// * `theory_name` — used only in the `<title>` (not entity-escaped here since
23	///   the observed value is a plain identifier; escape upstream if needed).
24	/// * `dotsrc` — full path to the DOT source, e.g.
25	///   `/thy/trace/3/interactive-graph-def/proof/exec`.
26	pub fn render_intdot(theory_name: &str, dotsrc: &str) -> String {
27	    INTDOT_TEMPLATE
28	        .replace("§NAME§", theory_name)
29	        .replace("§DOTSRC§", dotsrc)
30	}
31	
32	/// Convenience: build the `dotsrc` path for a given index and trailing proof
33	/// path segments (everything after `interactive-graph-def/`).
34	pub fn dotsrc_path(index: u64, trailing: &str) -> String {
35	    format!("/thy/trace/{index}/interactive-graph-def/{trailing}")
36	}

---

## 13. Handler semantics — the UI state machine (Round 3)

Derived from live probing of the sanctioned oracle ([L7]–[L16], Tutorial.spthy,
ports 3137-3141) plus corpus route/link census ([Q029]–[Q035]). This section
pins down *what each route returns and how theory-version state evolves*; the
per-body bytes are §§1–12. Reproduced in `src/dispatch.rs` over a `ProverOps`
callback trait (the prover supplies fragments/mutations; the web layer decides
version allocation, route dispatch, and the response envelope).

### 13.1 Version model (spec item 1)
* **Version 1** = the theory as loaded ("Original"); higher indices are
  "Modified". Every version stays **resolvable by index** forever, even after it
  scrolls out of the index-page table.
* **Proof operations** — `main/method/{lemma}/{n}[/path…]` and `autoprove/…` —
  allocate a **fresh** index `= (max ever allocated) + 1` (monotonic global
  counter, independent of the base index), leaving the base untouched ([L8],[L9]).
* **Structural edits** — POST `edit/{edit,add,delete}/{name}` — mutate the theory
  **in place at the same index** (no new version) ([L12]).
* **Navigation / views** (`overview`, `main/*` reads, `next`, `prev`, `source`,
  `intdot`, `interactive-graph-def`) never change the version set ([L10]).
* The index page lists the Original row plus a **capped window** of the most
  recent Modified rows (= the manifests' `capped` flag); dropped rows still
  resolve ([L11]). Row `Time`/`Origin` are non-deterministic (§7).

### 13.2 autoprove variants (spec item 2)
Route: `autoprove/{strategy}/{bound}/{allSol}/proof/{lemma}[/path…]`.
* `strategy` ∈ {`idfs` (solve/prove), `characterize` (characterization — e.g.
  exists-trace / observational goals)} ([Q030]).
* `bound` — a numeric depth bound; observed `0` (unbounded) and `5` (bounded).
* `allSol` ∈ {`False`, `True`}.
Keyboard-help mapping ([Q031], the "a/b/all/characterization" matrix):

| key | meaning | route projection |
|-----|---------|------------------|
| `a` | autoprove focused step, stop after first solution | `idfs/0/False` |
| `A` | …search for **all** solutions | `idfs/0/True` |
| `b` | **bounded**-depth autoprove, stop at first | `idfs/5/False` |
| `B` | bounded, all solutions | `idfs/5/True` |
| `s`/`S` | autoprove **all** lemmas (stop / all) | idfs, per-lemma |
| — | characterization | `characterize/{0,5}/{False,True}` |

So "all" = the `True` (all-solutions) flag and "characterization" = the
`characterize` strategy. **Response:** HTTP `200` + JSON
`{"redirect":"/thy/trace/{new}/overview/proof/{lemma}/{focus}"}`, `{new}` the
freshly allocated index and `{focus}` the prover's resulting focus path ([L9]).

### 13.3 Proof-step application, del, add (spec item 3)
* **Apply method** `GET main/method/{lemma}/{n}[/path…]` — the method number
  precedes the case-name path ([Q032]). Response = `200` + JSON
  `{"redirect":"/thy/trace/{new}/overview/proof/{lemma}/{focus}"}` ([L8]).
* **Delete lemma** `POST edit/delete/{name}` — `303 See Other`, `Location:
  /thy/trace/{v}/overview/help`, empty body, in place ([L12]).
* **Edit lemma** `POST edit/edit/{name}` (form field `lemma-text`) — on success
  `303` → `overview/edit/{name}`; on parse/wf failure `200` re-rendering the
  full-page **edit form** (theory unchanged) ([L12],[L13]).
* **Add lemma** `POST edit/add/{pos}` (`lemma-text`) — on success `303` →
  `overview/add/{pos}`; failure `200` add-form page. `{pos}` is a lemma name or
  `%3Cfirst%3E` ([L12]). These `overview/edit|add/…` full pages appear ONLY as
  POST-redirect targets (not in the crawl).

### 13.4 Proof-tree traversal & path encoding (spec item 1)
* `GET next|prev/{mode}/proof/{lemma}` → `200` `text/plain`, a **bare URL** at the
  **same** version. Target is the prover's `nextThyPath`-style computation: an
  adjacent proof node (`main/proof/{lemma}[/path]`) or a non-proof node
  (`main/cases/refined/0/0` before the first lemma); the last lemma's `next` is
  itself ([L10]). `mode` (`normal` in the corpus; `smart`/others accepted live)
  is passed opaquely to the prover.
* **Proof path** = raw `/`-join of case-name segments, root marker `_`. Segments
  are prover identifiers `[A-Za-z0-9_]` (up to ~112 chars observed); **no
  percent-encoding** anywhere in proof paths ([Q034]). The redirect focus after a
  proof op already includes the leading `_`.

### 13.5 Integration blocker (a): non-local page shell — NEGATIVE RESULT
Bound the oracle to all IPv4 interfaces (`--interface=*4`, `=` form; the space
form hits the workdir-positional bug) and fetched every route BOTH via loopback
`127.0.0.1` and via the machine's non-loopback `212.100.173.110` (the server LOG
confirms the peer address is `212.100.173.110`). Result: **byte-identical** on
every route — read views, forms, index `/`, 404, and mutating GET/POST (method,
edit, delete, reload all succeed and match). Host header
(`127.0.0.1`/`localhost`/`212.x`/`evil.com`) and `X-Forwarded-For`/`X-Real-IP`/
`X-Forwarded-Host` have **no** effect ([L15]). => In oracle **1.13.0** the served
shell is **origin-independent**. The locality predicate evidently treats all of
the machine's own interface addresses (loopback + `212.x`) as local, so a foreign
peer cannot be synthesised on a single host; the non-local shell's bytes are
**unobservable here**. The renderer is therefore parameterised over an
`origin_local` hook (see §13.7 / REPORT3) for the ported prover to drive, with
the exact non-local bytes left as a documented gap pending a multi-host capture.

### 13.6 Integration blocker (b): edit-form textarea `rows=` — SOLVED
`rows = (count of '\n' in the raw lemma source) + 2`. Verified over 4 lemmas
(newlines 9/11/7/10 → rows 11/13/9/12) ([L14]); HTML-escaping preserves newline
count, so it is computed on the raw text. Implemented as `forms::edit_rows`,
replacing the round-1 hardcoded `rows="8"` (a single-capture constant flagged by
the similarity audit). The **add** form's textarea has no `rows` attribute (fixed
placeholder `Enter your new Lemma`).

### 13.7 Content-types & the ProverOps boundary
Content-types ([L6]/verified [L16]): JSON `application/json; charset=utf-8`; HTML
`text/html; charset=utf-8`; text, `next`/`prev`, and **DOT**
`text/plain; charset=utf-8`. Proof ops answer `200` (JSON body), structural POSTs
`303` (Location header). `src/dispatch.rs` implements the `Server<T: ProverOps>`
state machine: it owns the version map + monotonic counter and makes all of the
above decisions; `ProverOps` supplies parse/edit/add/delete, apply-method,
autoprove, next/prev target, and the opaque pretty-printed fragments (west pane,
center content, lemma source, source text, DOT).
EOF

input.content:
# REPORT3.md — handler semantics (the interactive-UI state machine)

Round 3 of the `weblayer` clean-room cluster. Prior rounds reproduced the
response **bodies** (JSON envelopes, page shells, proof-script pane, forms, DOT,
404) byte-for-byte. This round adds the **behaviour**: what each route *returns*
and how the theory-version state evolves — the UI state machine — parameterised
over a `ProverOps` callback trait the ported prover supplies at integration.

Everything below is derived from black-box observation only: the 81 crawl
manifests in `oracle/captured_responses/` (captured OUTPUT) and live probing of
the sanctioned oracle binary ([L7]–[L16], Tutorial.spthy, ports 3137-3141). No
file under `/home/kamilner/tamarin-rs/` was read; the only tamarin-rs touch was
executing the sanctioned binary (incl. `interactive --help` and
`--interface=*4`, extra CLI flags the protocol permits).

## Deliverables

* `src/dispatch.rs` (new, 370 LOC) — the `Server<T: ProverOps>` state machine:
  route dispatch, theory-version management, response-envelope assembly.
* `src/route.rs` — grammar refined: `main/method/{lemma}/{n}[/path…]` now carries
  the proof path (the number precedes it); structured parsers `Autoprove`, `Nav`,
  `OverviewView`, `EditVerb` for the dispatcher.
* `src/forms.rs` — `edit_rows` implements the real textarea `rows=` formula
  (blocker b), replacing the round-1 hardcoded `rows="8"`.
* `tests/dispatch.rs` (15 tests) + `tests/fixtures/r3_*` — capture-backed
  behaviour/byte-parity tests.
* `BEHAVIOR.md` §13, `QUERIES.log` [Q029]–[L16].

`cargo test` → **55 passing** (21 unit + 15 dispatch + 19 parity), `cargo clippy
--tests --examples` clean.

## The state machine (`ProverOps` + `Server`)

The web layer owns exactly two pieces of state: a `BTreeMap<u64, Theory>` of
theory versions and a monotonic `next_index`. `ProverOps` is the prover boundary
— parse/edit/add/delete a lemma, apply a proof method at a path, autoprove, the
next/prev traversal target, and the opaque pretty-printed fragments (west pane,
center content, lemma source, theory source, DOT). `Server::dispatch(req)` parses
the route, resolves the numeric index, and makes every web-layer decision:

### 1. Version increments (spec item 1)
* **Proof ops** (`main/method`, `autoprove`) allocate a **fresh** index
  `= (max ever allocated)+1` and leave the base version intact; every version
  stays resolvable ([L8],[L9],[L11]).
* **Structural edits** (POST `edit/{edit,add,delete}`) mutate the theory **in
  place** at the requested index — no new version ([L12]).
* Navigation/views never change the version set ([L10]).

Byte evidence: `GET main/method/Client_session_key_secrecy/1` on v1 →
`{"redirect":"/thy/trace/2/overview/proof/Client_session_key_secrecy/_"}`
(identical to capture `r3_method_redirect.json`); a second identical request →
`/thy/trace/3/…`; `POST edit/delete/Client_auth` → `303`, no new index.

### 2. autoprove variants (spec item 2)
Route `autoprove/{strategy}/{bound}/{allSol}/proof/{lemma}[/path…]`. The
keyboard-help "a/b/all/characterization" matrix ([Q031]) decodes as: `strategy` ∈
{`idfs` prove, `characterize`}; `bound` `0`=unbounded(`a`/`A`), `5`=bounded
(`b`/`B`); `allSol` `False`=stop-at-first(lowercase), `True`=all-solutions
("all", uppercase). Every variant answers `200` + JSON
`{"redirect":"/thy/trace/{new}/overview/proof/{lemma}/{focus}"}` at a new version
([L9]). `Autoprove::parse` recovers the full matrix from the URL (tested).

### 3. proof-step / del / add (spec item 3)
* `main/method/{lemma}/{n}[/path…]` — number **before** the case-name path
  ([Q032]); `200` JSON redirect, new version.
* `POST edit/delete/{name}` → `303` `Location: …/overview/help`, empty body, in
  place.
* `POST edit/edit/{name}` (`lemma-text`) → success `303` `…/overview/edit/{name}`;
  **failure** `200` re-rendering the full edit-form page, theory unchanged
  ([L13]).
* `POST edit/add/{pos}` → success `303` `…/overview/add/{pos}`; failure `200`
  add-form page. `overview/edit|add/…` are full pages that appear only as these
  POST-redirect targets.

### 4. Traversal & path encoding (spec item 1)
`next|prev/{mode}/proof/{lemma}` → `200` `text/plain` bare URL at the same
version (`nav_target` is a prover computation; `mode` opaque). Proof paths are the
raw `/`-join of case-name segments (root `_`), segments `[A-Za-z0-9_]`, **no
percent-encoding** ([Q034]).

## Integration blockers

### (b) edit-form textarea `rows=` — SOLVED
`rows = (#'\n' in the raw lemma source) + 2`, verified over 4 lemmas
(9/11/7/10 → 11/13/9/12, [L14]). Implemented as `forms::edit_rows`;
`edit_form_envelope_byte_parity_with_rows_formula` reproduces the whole captured
edit-form JSON envelope for two lemmas (rows 11 and 9) byte-for-byte.

### (a) non-local page shell — NEGATIVE RESULT (environment-limited)
The spec expected the served shell to differ for a non-`127.0.0.1` client. I
bound the oracle to all IPv4 interfaces (`--interface=*4`) and fetched every route
BOTH via `127.0.0.1` and via the machine's non-loopback `212.100.173.110` — the
server access-log **confirms** the peer is `212.100.173.110`. Result: **every
route is byte-identical** (read views, forms, index, 404, and mutating GET/POST —
method/edit/delete/reload all succeed and match). Host and

## The state machine (`ProverOps` + `Server`)

The web layer owns exactly two pieces of state: a `BTreeMap<u64, Theory>` of
theory versions and a monotonic `next_index`. `ProverOps` is the prover boundary
- parse/edit/add/delete a lemma, apply a proof method at a path, autoprove, the
next/prev traversal target, and the opaque pretty-printed fragments (west pane,

the route, resolves the numeric index, and makes every web-layer decision:

### 1. Version increments (spec item 1)
* **Proof ops** (`main/method`, `autoprove`) allocate a **fresh** index
  `= (max ever allocated)+1` and leave the base version intact; every version
  stays resolvable ([L8],[L9],[L11]).
* **Structural edits** (POST `edit/{edit,add,delete}`) mutate the theory **in
  place** at the requested index - no new version ([L12]).
* Navigation/views never change the version set ([L10]).

### 2. autoprove variants (spec item 2)

keyboard-help "a/b/all/characterization" matrix ([Q031]) decodes as: `strategy` in
